bibliographic.citation: Max Förster, ‘Die älteste Fassung des me. Gedichtes “Earth upon Earth”,’ Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 138 (Neue Serie 38; 73. Jahrgang) (1919): 39–61.
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen
bibliographic.publisher: Druck und Verlag von Georg Westermann
bibliographic.title: Die älteste Fassung des me. Gedichtes 'Earth upon Earth'

entities.label: Max Förster, Die älteste Fassung des me. Gedichtes 'Earth upon Earth' (1919)
notes: CME00138 begins at article p. 50 and ends at p. 61.
Förster's five-stanza Urform is an editorial reconstruction, not a transcription of a single witness without intervention.

rights.basis: Published in 1919, before the United States 1931 public-domain cutoff applicable in 2026; this does not determine rights in modern scans or repository encodings.
rights.component: 1919 article text and typography as historical publication

access.accessMethod: Contact Special Collections for consultation or a quote for complete physical-object imaging, separately identifying ff. 44r–45r.
access.entityLabel: St John's College Cambridge MS E.24

entities.description: Förster's newly used witness. The opening couplet and first five B-version stanzas are on f. 44r; a following nine-stanza poem continues through f. 45r. Förster treats the latter as an independent poem, whereas DIMEV 6369 describes one fourteen-quatrain B-version witness.
entities.label: St John's College Cambridge MS E.24

access.accessMethod: No present repository or confirmed survival was established; trace the 1893 Bateman sale and later ownership before seeking images.

entities.description: A parchment roll formerly owned by William Yates and later the Bateman family at Lomberdale House. Förster reports a probable 1893 Sotheby sale but says Napier and Murray could not locate it.
entities.label: William Yates–Bateman parchment roll

access.accessMethod: Request complete physical-object imaging or onsite access, separately identifying f. 279r–v; the public selected image does not overlap.

entities.description: A seven-stanza text painted on an angel's scroll on the west wall of the nave. Current archaeology reports that the mural survives and was re-exposed in 1956, correcting Förster's statement that it had been destroyed.

notes: The painting is associated with the late-fifteenth-century chapel scheme; surviving text and upper areas have losses.

access.accessMethod: Order the open original at Kew; Discovery marks it not digitised.

entities.description: A four-membrane Exchequer roll whose principal Ordinances material dates to the fourteenth century. The trilingual Earth upon Earth poem was added later at the top of membrane 4d; the record's 1311–1312 date belongs to the principal legal content, not securely to the poem.
entities.label: The National Archives E 175/11/16

notes: Harrington dates the later hand that filled blanks to the later fourteenth or early fifteenth century.

entities.description: The principal witness of Murray's A version, seven six-line aaaabb stanzas at ff. 62r–63v. Förster prints only its first stanza through Murray.
entities.label: British Library Harley MS 913

next_steps: Inspect ff. 44r–45r directly, including layout, rubrics, hands, and any Latin/French parallels.
Compare Förster 1919, Murray 1911, DIMEV v1.0, Harrington 2016, and Nancy Pope's current edition/work analysis.
notes: This manifest preserves both analyses and does not force the second poem into the B-version work identity.
openQuestions.question: Should the nine stanzas following the first five B-version stanzas in St John's E.24 be cataloged as a distinct work, as Förster argues, or as one fourteen-quatrain B-version witness, as DIMEV 6369 does?

next_steps: Obtain the current College catalog description or images of the target and foliation sequence.
Locate Napier's working transcript and any annotations recording the old foliation.
openQuestions.question: What codicological or refoliation history explains Förster's Oxford Corpus Christi MS 237 folio 233v versus DIMEV's current f. 240v?

next_steps: Search Napier and Zupitza papers and correspondence.
Collate Förster's printed Cp text against current f. 240v images when obtainable.
openQuestions.question: Does A. S. Napier's working transcript of Corpus Christi MS 237 survive, and where are its precise diplomatic/editorial features documented?

next_steps: Identify the Sotheby Bateman sale catalog and trace lots and buyers.
Search manuscript sale/provenance databases using the incipit and Yates/Bateman/Lomberdale history.
openQuestions.question: Where is the Yates–Bateman parchment roll now, and did it survive the reported 1893 sale?

next_steps: Trace Förster's citations and Zupitza/Napier papers witness by witness.
Retain Murray as the documented general intermediary until a more exact route is established.
openQuestions.question: Beyond St John's E.24 and Napier's Cp transcript, which original manuscripts, working transcripts, or earlier editions did Förster directly consult for individual apparatus readings?

next_steps: Consult current work-specific linguistic, textual, and manuscript scholarship.
Keep composition date/place separate from St John's witness date and dialect unless the inference is independently supported.
openQuestions.question: Can Förster's tentative thirteenth-century and northern or North-Midland hypothesis for the reconstructed five-stanza Urform be supported independently of witness age and selected linguistic forms?

next_steps: Inspect current f. 59v and adjacent foliation when BL digital access returns.
Compare the 1911 print/frontispiece and earlier editions cited by DIMEV.
openQuestions.question: What historical foliation or printing error produced Förster's Harley 2253 f. 57v citation for the four-line A-version parallel now cataloged at f. 59v?

next_steps: Prioritize St John's E.24, Oxford CCC 237, TNA E 175/11/16, Lambeth 853, Rawlinson poet. 32, and the BL target leaves.
Request whole physical objects rather than target crops when feasible and record exact supply/reuse terms.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable physical-object facsimiles be obtained for the witnesses now represented only by catalog records, requests, unavailable viewers, or zero-overlap selections?

reviewNotes: Förster's twenty-three B-version sigla denote textual witnesses used in the critical apparatus; they do not establish that he personally inspected twenty-three physical objects.

DIMEV v1.0 treats St John's E.24 ff. 44r–45r as one fourteen-quatrain B-version witness, while Förster separates the first five stanzas from the following nine-stanza poem. The conflict is preserved rather than resolved silently.

Förster's folio 233v for Oxford Corpus Christi MS 237 conflicts with the current DIMEV locator 240v; his Harley 2253 folio 57v conflicts with the current 59v locator. Both historical and current locators are retained.

Förster called the Stratford Guild Chapel mural destroyed. Current archaeological reporting describes the painting as extant, re-exposed in 1956, and among the chapel's best-preserved schemes, although upper and textual portions have suffered loss.
Access and rights statements apply only to the named entity or component. Public viewing never by itself licenses bulk download or redistribution.
Negative facsimile findings are date-bounded to 2026-07-13 and do not prove that no private, paid, or unindexed reproduction exists.

lineage.summary: The repository XML copies University of Michigan CME item CME00138 and encodes only printed pages 50–61 of Max Förster's 1919 article, not the complete article on pages 39–61. Those pages form an edited selection rather than one medieval work: an eclectic five-stanza B-version reconstruction with an introductory couplet, two groups of added stanzas, a separate seven-stanza Oxford Corpus Christi text transmitted through A. S. Napier's working transcript, an independent nine-stanza poem from St John's E.24 collated through Murray against a Public Record Office roll, and a one-stanza sample of Murray's A version. Förster's inherited B-version readings are routed through Hilda Murray's 1911 edition rather than treated as direct physical consultation. Complete auditable facsimile objects are cached for Trinity R.3.21, Penn Oversize MS Codex 721, Brogyntyn II.1, Selden Supra 53, Balliol 354, and the current Trinity B.7.4 host of former B.15.39; selected-image caches with zero target overlap are explicitly partial.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous historical poets

catalogSummary.title: Die älteste Fassung des me. Gedichtes 'Earth upon Earth'

catalogingSubject.label: Die älteste Fassung des me. Gedichtes 'Earth upon Earth'
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The repository item is Max Förster's article-level edited selection on printed pp. 50–61, not one medieval poem. It combines German editorial argument and apparatus with a reconstructed five-stanza B-version core and introductory couplet, two blocks of shared additional B-version stanzas, a complete seven-stanza Corpus Christi College MS 237 text mediated by Arthur Napier's transcript, an independent nine-stanza poem from St John's College MS E.24 collated against the Public Record Office roll, and only the first stanza of Murray's A version. Abstract works and versions, physical witnesses, intermediary transcripts, Förster's 1919 edition, the Michigan encoding, and the repository artifact remain distinct layers.

contentParts.label: Förster's editorial frame and critical apparatus

locators.value: BODY headings, HEADNOTE, NOTE, DATELINE, and prose P elements across DIV1[1]–DIV1[3]

notes: item_count 0 records that this frame is not a separately numbered historical text while retaining it in prose/verse extent totals.
contentParts.summary: Noncontiguous German headings, headnotes, witness descriptions, textual argument, line and stanza commentary, and variant notes surrounding all selected texts.

contentParts.label: Five-stanza B-version Urform reconstruction
notes: The heading 'Urform' expresses Förster's reconstruction, not a surviving authorial archetype or a single diplomatic transcription.
contentParts.summary: Förster's reconstructed early B-version core: a two-line English introductory epigraph followed by five monorhyming quatrains, with a Latin heading and extensive multi-witness apparatus.

contentParts.label: Shared added stanzas of the second manuscript group
contentParts.summary: Two four-line stanzas that Förster treats as a shared secondary accretion in a manuscript group of the B-version tradition.

contentParts.label: Shared added stanzas of the third manuscript group
contentParts.summary: Five four-line stanzas that Förster treats as a further shared accretion in a third manuscript group of the B-version tradition.

contentParts.label: Seven-stanza Oxford Corpus Christi College MS 237 text

locators.value: current f. 240v; Förster's old foliation f. 233v

notes: Napier's transcript is modeled as an intermediary in the lineage manifest.
contentParts.summary: A complete seven-quatrain B-version witness text printed by Förster from Arthur Napier's transcript rather than from direct inspection of the codex.

contentParts.label: Independent nine-stanza poem beginning 'Whan erþe hauet erþe wiþ woo by-ȝute'
notes: Direct main text comprises nine LG and 36 L; the structural metrics include the one LG/four L Public Record Office-only apparatus stanza nested inside the fourth stanza's note.
contentParts.summary: Förster's edition of a formally independent nine-quatrain poem from St John's College MS E.24, collated against the Public Record Office roll; the apparatus also embeds one four-line stanza found only in the roll.

contentParts.label: First-stanza sample of Murray's A version

locators.value: ff. 62r–63v; Förster prints only the first stanza

contentParts.summary: Only the first six-line aaaabb stanza of the seven-stanza A version, printed by Förster through Hilda M. R. Murray's 1911 edition.

notes: This dates the surviving witness, not either work's composition.

scope.description: The relevant B-version opening/core and following independent poem as attested in St John's College MS E.24

notes: The current catalog date supersedes Förster's broader early-fourteenth-century description and remains witness-level evidence.

scope.description: Complete A-version witness in Harley MS 913, from which Förster prints one stanza through Murray
Publication of Förster's edited article selection, not composition of the medieval verse

extent.description: The XML has three DIV1 sections, two DIV2, three DIV3, 12 PB, 146 NOTE, 1,192 HI, two MILESTONE, 29 direct main-text LG, and 120 direct main-text L. A four-line Public Record Office-only stanza is nested as one LG inside a NOTE; counting that structurally encoded apparatus stanza gives 30 LG and 124 L. The two-line English epigraph before the reconstructed B-version core contributes two L but no LG. Six historical verse units are counted as numbered items; the discontinuous editorial frame has item_count 0 but contributes one prose-only component.

formStatements.description: German prose commentary and apparatus surrounding Middle English verse texts

scope.description: Historical verse selected or reconstructed by Förster
Latin heading before the reconstructed B-version core and Latin readings or paraphrases quoted in the apparatus

notes: The cataloging subject is the article-level edited selection encoded on Förster's pp. 50–61, not the complete 1919 article on pp. 39–61 and not one medieval authored poem.
The 1919 publication date and German editorial language are scoped to Förster's edition layer and excluded from unqualified medieval-work discovery facets.
Witness dates, origins, dialects, present holdings, and foliation are not composition facts for the abstract works or versions.
Structural verse totals include the four-line Public Record Office-only stanza nested inside a NOTE; direct main-text totals remain 29 LG and 120 L.
Förster's tentative thirteenth-century, northern or North-Midlands Urform hypothesis remains an open research question rather than a date or place statement.
The main Ordinances on TNA E 175/11/16 date to 1311–12, but the Earth-upon-Earth material is a later addition on membrane 4d; the administrative document date is not transferred to the poem.

next_steps: Obtain and inspect complete images of ff. 44r–45r, including layout, headings, spacing, and scribal transitions.
Compare the formal, lexical, and transmission evidence used by Förster with DIMEV's witness-level classification.
Retain both explicit models and avoid silently merging the two conceptual work units.
openQuestions.question: Should St John's College MS E.24, ff. 44r–45r, be modeled as Förster's five-stanza B-version core followed by an independent nine-stanza poem, or as DIMEV 6369's single fourteen-quatrain B-version witness with an introductory couplet?

next_steps: Reassess Förster's tentative thirteenth-century and North or North-Midlands hypothesis against later textual and linguistic scholarship.
Separate archetype reconstruction from the date and localization of each witness reading.
Add composition facets only when work-level evidence supports them.
openQuestions.question: Can a composition date or place be established for Förster's reconstructed five-stanza B-version core independently of the dates, origins, and dialects of its surviving witnesses?

next_steps: Collate each stanza's full witness distribution against Förster's sigla and current DIMEV witness assignments.
Compare meter, rhyme, diction, and devotional emphasis across the reconstructed core and added layers.
Do not assign the collective anonymous B-version responsibility to a single person without stronger evidence.
openQuestions.question: Do the two shared added-stanza blocks and the individual witness additions represent one reviser, several anonymous poets, or independent accretions?

notes: The added stanzas may represent more than one anonymous poet or reviser.

scope.description: The B-version core, shared additional stanza blocks, and the Corpus Christi College MS 237 B-version text

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous poet of 'Whan erþe hauet erþe wiþ woo by-ȝute'
Anonymous poet of the A version

scope.description: Selection and arrangement of reconstructed text, shared stanza blocks, complete witness text, independent poem, and A-version sample
B-version reconstruction and apparatus, and collation of the independent St John's text against the Public Record Office roll

subjects.label: Transience of the world and worldly vanity

scope.description: Förster's reconstruction, grouping of stanza layers, witness apparatus, and cross-version comparison

summaries.value: Förster's edited selection reconstructs an early five-stanza B-version core of 'Erthe upon Erthe', groups two successive bodies of shared added stanzas, prints a complete seven-stanza Oxford Corpus Christi College text, edits a formally independent nine-stanza 'Whan erþe' poem from St John's College MS E.24 with variants from a Public Record Office roll, and samples the A version. The verse repeatedly figures the human body as earth in order to oppose worldly rank and possession to death, burial, judgment, repentance, and salvation.

Seven modeled parts: a noncontiguous German editorial frame; a reconstructed B-version core of five quatrains plus a two-line English introductory epigraph; two shared added-stanza blocks of two and five quatrains; a seven-quatrain Corpus Christi College MS 237 text; a nine-quatrain independent poem plus one four-line Public Record Office variant stanza nested in its apparatus; and one six-line sample stanza of Murray's A version.

The catalog record describes Förster's edited selection as a whole while preserving six historical verse units as parts. The 1919 publication date and German editorial language characterize the edition layer; manuscript dates, loci, origins, and dialects characterize witnesses. None supplies an unqualified composition date, place, or author for all selected medieval material.

notes: Förster's exact article title; it identifies the edited selection rather than supplying one medieval uniform title.
titles.value: Die älteste Fassung des me. Gedichtes 'Earth upon Earth'
The Earliest Version of the Middle English Poem 'Earth upon Earth'

notes: The reconstructed stanza text follows a separately printed two-line introductory epigraph.

work.displayTitle: The Earliest Version of the Middle English Poem 'Earth upon Earth'
work.preferredTitle: Die älteste Fassung des me. Gedichtes 'Earth upon Earth'

work.regionDisplay: Unknown; witness origins and dialects are not transferred to the abstract works

subjects.label: Transience of the world and worldly vanity

work.summary: Förster's edited selection reconstructs an early five-stanza B-version core of 'Erthe upon Erthe', groups two successive bodies of shared added stanzas, prints a complete seven-stanza Oxford Corpus Christi College text, edits a formally independent nine-stanza 'Whan erþe' poem from St John's College MS E.24 with variants from a Public Record Office roll, and samples the A version. The verse repeatedly figures the human body as earth in order to oppose worldly rank and possession to death, burial, judgment, repentance, and salvation.
work.translatedTitle: The Earliest Version of the Middle English Poem 'Earth upon Earth'
